title: Vincent of Beauvais

infobox.value: Vincentius Bellovacensis, Vincentius Burgundus, Vincent de Beauvais

([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Vincentius Bellovacensis* or *Burgundus*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Vincent de Beauvais*; c. 1184/1194 – c. 1264) was a [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") friar at the [Cistercian](/wiki/Cistercian "Cistercian") monastery of [Royaumont Abbey](/wiki/Royaumont_Abbey "Royaumont Abbey"), [France](/wiki/France "France").[[1]](#cite_note-1) He is known mostly for his *[Speculum Maius](/wiki/Speculum_Maius "Speculum Maius")* (*Great mirror*), a major work of compilation that was widely read in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). Often retroactively described as an [encyclopedia](/wiki/Encyclopedia "Encyclopedia") or as a *[florilegium](/wiki/Florilegium "Florilegium")*,[[2]](#cite_note-2) his text exists as a core example of brief compendiums produced in medieval Europe.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
["Vincent de Beauvais"](https://www.arlima.net/uz/vincent_de_beauvais.html) (in French). Arlima Archives de littérature du moyen âge. Retrieved January 29, 2018.

Franklin-Brown, Mary (2012). *Reading the world : encyclopedic writing in the scholastic age*. Chicago London: The University of Chicago Press. p. 9. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780226260709](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780226260709 "Special:BookSources/9780226260709").

Binkley, Peter (1997). *Pre-modern encyclopaedic texts : proceedings of the second COMERS Congress, Groningen, 1-4 July 1996*. Leiden New York: Brill. p. 48. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9789004108301](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9789004108301 "Special:BookSources/9789004108301").

Jacobs-Pollez, Rebecca (2012). *The education of noble girls in medieval france: Vincent of beauvais and "De eruditione filiorum nobilium"* (PhD). University of Missouri - Columbia. p. 234. [ProQuest](/wiki/ProQuest "ProQuest") [1266044945](https://www.proquest.com/docview/1266044945).

outgoing_links.text: Theobald V of Champagne and Navarre

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

13th-century French encyclopedist

| Vincent of Beauvais | |
| --- | --- |
| [Fresco of Friar Vincent of Beauvais, author of The Great Mirror](/wiki/File:Vincent_de_Beauvais.jpg) | |
| Born | c. 1184/1194 |
| Died | c. 1264 |
| Other names | Vincentius Bellovacensis, Vincentius Burgundus, Vincent de Beauvais |
| Occupation | Friar |
| Known for | Author/Compiler |
| Notable work | *The Great Mirror* (*Speculum Maius*) |

**Vincent of Beauvais** ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Vincentius Bellovacensis* or *Burgundus*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Vincent de Beauvais*; c. 1184/1194 – c. 1264) was a [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") friar at the [Cistercian](/wiki/Cistercian "Cistercian") monastery of [Royaumont Abbey](/wiki/Royaumont_Abbey "Royaumont Abbey"), [France](/wiki/France "France").[[1]](#cite_note-1) He is known mostly for his *[Speculum Maius](/wiki/Speculum_Maius "Speculum Maius")* (*Great mirror*), a major work of compilation that was widely read in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). Often retroactively described as an [encyclopedia](/wiki/Encyclopedia "Encyclopedia") or as a *[florilegium](/wiki/Florilegium "Florilegium")*,[[2]](#cite_note-2) his text exists as a core example of brief compendiums produced in medieval Europe.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vincent_of_Beauvais&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

The exact dates of his birth and death are unknown, and not much detail has surfaced concerning his career. Conjectures place him first in the house of the [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") at [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") between 1215 and 1220, and later at the Dominican monastery founded by [Louis IX of France](/wiki/Louis_IX_of_France "Louis IX of France") at [Beauvais](/wiki/Beauvais "Beauvais") in [Picardy](/wiki/Picardy "Picardy"). It is more certain, however, that he held the post of "reader" at the monastery of [Royaumont](/wiki/Royaumont "Royaumont") on the [Oise](/wiki/Oise "Oise"), not far from Paris, also founded by Louis IX, between 1228 and 1235. Around the late 1230s, Vincent had begun working on the *Great Mirror* and in 1244 he had completed the first draft.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The king read the books that Vincent compiled and supplied the funds for procuring copies of such authors as he required. Queen [Margaret of Provence](/wiki/Margaret_of_Provence "Margaret of Provence") and her son-in-law, [Theobald V of Champagne and Navarre](/wiki/Theobald_V_of_Champagne "Theobald V of Champagne"), are also named among those who urged him to the composition of his "little works", especially *De morali principis institutione*.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArcher191190-5) In the late 1240s, Vincent was working on his *Opus* which included *On the Education of Noble Girls* (*De Eruditione Filiorum Nobilium*). In this work he styles himself as "Vincentius Belvacensis, de ordine praedicatorum, qualiscumque lector in monasterio de Regali Monte".[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArcher191190-5) Though Vincent may have been summoned to Royaumont before 1240, there is no evidence that he lived there before the return of Louis IX and his wife from the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land "Holy Land").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArcher191190-5) It is possible that he left Royaumont in 1260,[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez2012235-6) which is also the approximate year that he wrote *Tractatus Consolatorius*, which was occasioned by the death of the king's son Louis that year.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArcher191190-5) Between the years 1260 and 1264 Vincent sent the first completed book of the *Opus* to Louis IX and Thibaut V.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez2012235-6) In 1264 he died.

## *Great Mirror* (*Speculum Maius*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vincent_of_Beauvais&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Great Mirror (Speculum Maius)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/BL_Royal_Vincent_of_Beauvais.jpg/250px-BL_Royal_Vincent_of_Beauvais.jpg)](/wiki/File:BL_Royal_Vincent_of_Beauvais.jpg)

Miniature of Vincent of Beauvais in a manuscript of the *Speculum Historiale*, translated into French by Jean de Vignay, Bruges, c. 1478–1480, [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library") Royal 14 E. i, vol. 1, f. 3

What is known of Vincent and his historical importance largely depends on his compendium *[Speculum Maius](/wiki/Speculum_Maius "Speculum Maius")* or the *Great Mirror*. He worked on it for approximately 29 years (1235–1264)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranklin-Brown201211-7) in the pursuit of presenting a compendium of all of the knowledge available at the time. He collected the materials for the work from [Île-de-France](/wiki/%C3%8Ele-de-France "Île-de-France") libraries, and there is evidence to suggest even further than that.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranklin-Brown201295-8) He found support for the creation of the *Great Mirror* from the [Dominican order](/wiki/Dominican_order "Dominican order") to which he belonged as well as [King Louis IX](/wiki/King_Louis_IX "King Louis IX") of France.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranklin-Brown201295-8) The metaphor of the title has been argued to "reflect" the microcosmic relations of Medieval knowledge. In this case, the book mirrors "both the contents and organization of the cosmos".[[9]](#cite_note-9) Vincent himself stated that he chose "Speculum" for its name because his work contains "whatever is worthy of contemplation (*speculatio*), that is, admiration or imitation".[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranklin-Brown2012271-10) It is by this name that the compendium is connected to the medieval genre of [speculum literature](/wiki/Speculum_literature "Speculum literature").

## Other works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vincent_of_Beauvais&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Other works")]

* *Universal Work on the Royal Condition* (*Opus universale de statu principis*) was described as a guideline to "provide instructions for the behaviors and duties of the prince, his family, and his court".[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez2012iv-11) It was to be a four-part treatise, although only the first, *The moral instruction of a prince*, and the fourth, *The education of noble children*, were completed.

:   * *The Moral Instruction of a Prince* or *On the Foundations of Royal Morals* (*De morali Principis institutione*) (1260–1263), written for [King Louis IX](/wiki/King_Louis_IX "King Louis IX") of France on the topic of kingship.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
    * *The Education of Noble Children* (*De eruditione filiorum nobilium*) (1249).[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez2012234-13) This pedagogical treatise has also been viewed as a sermon expanding on the biblical passage Ecclesiasticus 7:25-26: "You have sons? Train them and care for them from boyhood. You have daughters? Guard their bodies and do not show a joyful face to them."[[14]](#cite_note-14) The majority of the text deals with the education of boys, with roughly one-fifth of the text devoted to the education of girls. The text is notable for being "the first medieval educational text to both systematically present a comprehensive method of instruction for lay children and to included a section devoted to girls."[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez2012iv-11)

* *Expositio in orationem dominicam* (*Exposition on the Lord’s Prayer*)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez201221-15)
* *Liber consolatorius ad Ludovicum regem de morte filii* (*Book to Console King Louis after the Death of His Son*), (1260)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez201221-15)
* *Liber de laudibus beatae Virginis* (*The Book Praising the Blessed Virgin*)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez201221-15)
* *Liber de laudibus Johannis Evangelistae* (*The Book Praising John the Evangelist*)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez201221-15)
* *Liber de sancta Trinitate* or *Tractatus de sancta trinitate* (*The Book of the Holy Trinity*), (1259–1260)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez2012234-13)
* *Liber gratiae* (*The Book of Grace*), (1259–1260)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez2012234-13)
* *Memoriale temporum* (*Chronicle of the Times*)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez201222-16)
* Sermones manuals de tempore. (Johann Koelhoff d. Ä., Cologne c. 1482 [digitized](http://digital.ub.uni-duesseldorf.de/urn/urn:nbn:de:hbz:061:1-371820))
* *Tractatus de poenitentia* (*Treatise on Penance*)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez201221-15)
* *Tractatus in salutatione beatae Virginis Mariae ab angelo facta* (*Treastie on the Salutation to the Blessed Virgin Mary Made by the Angel*)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez201221-15)

There are manuscript copies and modern editions of *De eruditione filiorum nobilium*, *De morali principis institutione*, *Liber consolatorius ad Ludovicum regem de morte filii*. There are only manuscript copies of *Liber de sancta Trinitate*, *Memoriale temporum*, *Tractatus de poenitentia*, and *Tractatus in salutatione beatae Virginis Mariae ab angelo facta*.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez2012236–237-17)

Beyond the thirteen works that can be confidently accredited to Vincent,[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez201220-18) there is the possibility of a lost work named *Tractatus de vitio detractionis* (*Treastise on the Sin of Omission*)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez201221-15) and the apocryphal fourth part to the *Great Mirror*, *Speculum morale*.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobs-Pollez2012237-19)

Along with [Conradus of Altzheim](/w/index.php?title=Conradus_of_Altzheim&action=edit&redlink=1 "Conradus of Altzheim (page does not exist)"), [Henricus Suso](/wiki/Henricus_Suso "Henricus Suso"), [Ludolphus of Saxony](/wiki/Ludolphus_of_Saxony "Ludolphus of Saxony"), the authorship of *[Speculum Humanae Salvationis](/wiki/Speculum_Humanae_Salvationis "Speculum Humanae Salvationis")* has been sometimes attributed to Vincent.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
